title: Hundreds hospitalised in Japan heatwave ‘disaster’ - Bangkok Post
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
full_text:
TOKYO - More than 450 people received hospital treatment for heat-related issues in a single day in Tokyo — the highest number since records began 16 years ago, emergency services said on Thursday.
The figure was released as Japan sweltered in a heatwave that has killed at least 14 people over the past week.
A total 453 people had hospital treatment for heat-related issues on Wednesday, the most since records began in 2010, the Tokyo Fire Department said. Emergency callouts were also at the highest level since at least 1963.
“We are urging people to stay hydrated and use air conditioners,” a fire brigade spokesman told AFP.
The number of patients appeared to rise as temperatures climbed, with notable increases on Tuesday and Wednesday, the spokesman said.
In Tokyo prefecture — home to around 14 million people — one person had died in the current heatwave with four others in a critical condition and 18 in a serious state, he said.
Nick Estes, 35, an American tourist visiting the Asakusa area of Tokyo with his family, said Thursday the weather was “extremely hot”.
“We’ve been planning our trips around trying to make sure we can spend time in the metro to cool off, finding little shops to pop into to maybe buy a little souvenir that has air conditioning, doing everything we can to stay cool,” Estes told AFP.
“But especially with younger kids, it’s been absolutely brutal… I was here last summer, actually, and somehow it actually feels even hotter this year than it did a year ago.”
The Fire and Disaster Management Agency has dubbed the heatwave a “disaster”, reporting on Wednesday at least 14 deaths across the country over the previous week and 11,000 people seeking emergency medical care.
‘Disaster’
In the central region of Aichi, which includes Nagoya and is experiencing some of the most intense heat, 116 people were taken to hospital on Wednesday, officials said.
Heatstroke alerts have been issued for almost the whole country of around 125 million people.
“Temperatures have been rising since mid-July, and severe heat is expected to continue nationwide,” the disaster agency said.
“It is no exaggeration to call this extreme heat a ‘disaster’ with many people being rushed to hospitals or even dying as a result of heatstroke,” it said.
On Thursday, at least six places experienced 40C temperatures or hotter by early afternoon, while the mercury rose to 39.9C in three other locations.
The hottest place was Hamamatsu in the central Shizuoka region at 41.1C.
It was followed by Toyota city — headquarters of the car giant — in the neighbouring Aichi region at 40.8C and Kuwana in Mie at 40.7C.
Japan’s highest recorded temperature was registered on Aug 5, 2025, at 41.8C in Isesaki, north of Tokyo.
Japan this year introduced this year a new term to describe heat above 40C called kokushobi, which translates to “cruelly hot day”.
The new term was designed to draw greater public attention to heat-related warnings, although it does not trigger any official measures nationwide.
‘Hotter and hotter’
Scientists say human-induced climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events in Japan and elsewhere.
Japan sweltered through its hottest summer on record last year.
“You leave the hotel at 8.30… it feels as if it was noontime. And as the day progresses, it just gets hotter and hotter and hotter,” said Yesenia Gueta, 47, visiting from Los Angeles.
“Now I understand why (Japanese) clothing is loose, why they wear the umbrellas all the time. I mean, it is extreme. And I agree with that term,” Gueta said.
“Since it’s like this in mid-July, I wonder what’s going to happen in August. I’m worried,” said Japanese university student Nukochama, 24.
“I carry a big fan like this, fully charged, so I can turn it on anytime.”

title: Extreme heat is becoming a bottom-line issue for business - Baton Rouge Business Report
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
full_text:
Extreme heat has become a persistent business challenge rather than a seasonal anomaly, with severe heat waves in North America now occurring about twice as often as they did in the mid-20th century, Bloomberg reports.
As temperatures continue to rise, extreme heat is straining infrastructure, increasing operating costs and forcing companies to adapt, although its effects vary across industries. Utilities are investing billions of dollars to modernize aging power grids that are increasingly vulnerable to surging air-conditioning demand, creating growth opportunities for manufacturers of transformers, cables, batteries and engineering services.
At the same time, the rapid expansion of AI-driven data centers has increased demand for advanced cooling technologies, benefiting companies that provide liquid cooling systems, specialized HVAC equipment and cooling management software. In the transportation sector, logistics companies are equipping trucks with air conditioning to improve driver safety and comply with new labor agreements and regulations, though these upgrades also increase fuel and operating costs.
Construction firms face some of the greatest challenges because outdoor work cannot always be protected from extreme heat. Reduced productivity, work stoppages and higher labor costs contributed to an estimated $38 billion in economic losses for the U.S. construction industry between 2001 and 2023.
Bloomberg has the full story.

title: Tunisia hit by protests as blackouts continue in 50C heatwave - The New Arab
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
full_text:
Summertime power cuts are commonplace in Tunisia, have spread across wider parts of the country this year [Getty/file photo]
Tunisia is witnessing a wave of angry protests across its cities in response to ongoing electricity and water cuts, as the North African country also experiences sweltering temperatures reaching up to 50 degrees Celsius (122°F).
In Menzel Abderrahmane in the Bizerte governorate, around 65 kilometres (40 miles) away from the capital Tunis, protesters set alight tyres and objects in the city centre on Wednesday night as anger over the cuts mounted.
These outages have caused partial disruptions to vital facilities and everyday life, and have also resulted in financial losses for residents and businesses alike.
Demonstrations have also taken place in Zaghouan, Nabeul, Monastir, and Sousse, and more.
In Ben Aoun, Governorate of Monastir, some demonstrators said that Tunisia's youth are on the verge of "another revolution" if the situation remains ongoing.
"Why are people out in the streets protesting? People use fridges, use oxygen, people are sick, people are tired," one man said in a video shared by Tunisian media.
Similar scenes could be seen in Zaghouan. Families were protesting on foot and in vehicles, blocking roads to voice their discontent with the ongoing cuts.
One hospital, in the northern city of Ariana, said on its Facebook page on Wednesday that it had received an influx of patients suffering from chronic respiratory failure, as a result of the heatwave and power cuts.
The Abderrahmane Mami Hospital said it was trying to "mobilise all human and logistical resources to ensure the immediate care of all arriving patients, while maintaining the continuity of health services and providing necessary care under optimal conditions," in response.
Demonstrators directed their anger towards President Kais Saied, accusing him of inaction as the power cuts and their impact on daily life have been ongoing for over a week.
In an official statement on Thursday, the Tunisian presidency said it was "monitoring the situation closely" and that a meeting was held with his cabinet on Wednesday to discuss the matter.
In the meeting, Saied reportedly stressed that these outages "must end immediately".
Saied has been the object of much opposition and protests since his tenure as president began in 2019. Tunisians have expressed discontent with the economic situation, rising inflation, as well as a stifling of dissent and critics.
Countless politicians, businessmen, journalists, and activists accused of "insulting" Saied or of "terrorism" have been jailed or put on trial since.
The power cuts, ongoing since 12 July, have come as the North African country is experiencing an intense heatwave where temperatures have reached 50 degrees Celsius in certain parts of the country.
The Tunisian Company of Electricity and Gas (STEG) has been implementing strategic power outages to help alleviate pressure on a grid struggling with high energy demand, as temperatures soar.
This measure is known as "rolling blackouts" - a term that has become all too familiar to Tunisians. The company maintains that this strategy prevents a total blackout that could plunge the country into darkness.
Tunisia has frequently experienced power cuts due to sweltering temperatures, but more specifically in the country’s more interior regions. This year, however, outages were reported across the country’s more major cities, triggering the angry demonstrations.
Tunisia’s electricity issues also stem from an overreliance on Algeria for electricity provision, as the country’s demand does not meet its domestic generation. It relies on interconnections with its neighbour for electricity imports, which reach around 10% of Tunisia's total electricity consumption.
In turn, cooperation with Algeria for electricity imports puts a strain on the country’s economy.

If you've got a trip to Spainbooked this summer, you'll want to keep an eye on the weather forecast. The country is facing its third official heatwave of 2026, with meteorologists warning of record-breaking temperatures in the coming days, on top of a wildfire season that's already proved deadly. Here's what's actually happening, and what it means for your travel plans.
What's happening in Spain?
Spain's national weather agency, AEMET, is forecasting an intensifying heatwave with temperatures expected to climb steadily from the weekend before peaking around Thursday. It follows what's already been an unusually hot summer, where average temperatures between early June and mid-July have been around 3.3C above the long-term norm, one of the warmest starts to summer on record in Spain.
Where in Spain will it be hottest?
The regions likely to see the highest temperatures are in the south, with Andalusia and popular destinations like Seville expected to be worst affected. Forecast models have suggested temperatures could reach as high as 47C or 48C in parts of the region, which would come close to or exceed Spain’s all-time record of 47.6C in Córdoba province in August 2021. Parts of Catalonia are also expected to see temperatures well above 40C over the coming days and popular tourist destinations like Malaga could see highs of 45C.
As ever with heatwave forecasts this far ahead, exact figures can change, so it's worth checking AEMET's official warnings closer to the time if you're travelling to an affected area.
One is in Aragón near Zaragoza, which has burnedan estimated 15,400 hectares since it broke out on July 15.The other is theLa Mierla firein Guadalajara province, Castilla-La Mancha, which has now burned over32,000 hectares. The fire has forced the evacuation of 34 municipalities, though regional services report that conditions are finally beginning to stabilise.
Beyond these two major fires, AEMET's wildfire risk maps continue to flag large parts of Aragón, Catalonia, Castilla y León, Galicia and Andalusia as being at high to extreme risk over the coming days.
The European Commission has deployed what it describes as its largest-ever wildfire response this year, pre-positioning 777 firefighters from 14 countries, along with 22 firefighting aircraft and 5 helicopters, across high-risk areas including Spain, France, Portugal, Italy, Greece and Cyprus.
Is it safe to travel?
As things stand, the FCDO has not advised against travel to any part of Spain because of the heatwave or wildfires, and there have been no reports of major airport disruption linked to the current fires. That said, conditions can change quickly during a heatwave, so it's worth checking your airline’s status page before you travel and keeping an eye on local news once you're there, particularly if you're headed to Andalusia, Aragón or Castilla-La Mancha.
If you're travelling during the hottest days, take the general precautions you would in any severe heat: avoid the sun between around 11am and 3pm where possible, stay hydrated, wear sunscreen and light clothing, and check on any vulnerable family members or travel companions. If you're heading into rural or wooded areas in an affected region, it's worth checking local wildfire risk maps first and following any evacuation orders immediately if they're issued.
If the FCDO hasn't advised against travel to your destination, you don't have an automatic right to cancel and claim your money back. Refunds and cancellations typically only become available if there's official government advice against travel to the area, a wildfire within a certain radius of your accommodation, or your flight or transport is cancelled as a direct result of a fire.
If you're concerned about an upcoming trip, contact your travel provider first, as some may offer flexibility even without a formal cancellation, particularly if you're headed somewhere close to an active fire. Cancelling purely out of caution where there's no official advice against travel makes a successful refund claim unlikely, so it's worth weighing up your options with your provider or travel insurer before doing so.

full_text:
Maksudangarh records 174mm rain;orange alert across several places
| Date :23-Jul-2026 |
Staff Reporter :
A RED alert has been issued for
isolated places in Chhindwara
district as an aggressive revival
of the monsoon triggers extremely heavy rainfall, severe thunderstorms, lightning, and highspeed gusty winds up to 40 km/h.
Concurrently, a stern orange
warning for very heavy rainfall
and gusty winds has been sounded for isolated locations across
Narmadapuram, Betul, Seoni,
and Pandhurna districts.
Leading the deluge over the last
24 hours, Maksudangarh recorded the State’s highest rainfall at
a staggering 174.0 mm. It was followed closely by Kumbhraj and
Jaisingh Nagar at 130.0 mm each,
Goharganj at 128.0 mm, and
Sultanpur at 122.0 mm.
Heavy downpours also battered Budhni (114.0 mm),
Narmadapuram (105.6 mm),
Pohri (103.0 mm), Rehti (84.2
mm), Berasia (82.2 mm),
Shamshabad (75.0 mm), Pachore
(74.0 mm), Sohagpur (73.0 mm),
Budhar (71.0 mm), Lalburra (70.3
mm), and Baihar (70.0 mm).
The widespread precipitation
brought a sharp plunge in day
temperatures across the state.
Maximum temperatures plummeted markedly by 4.8°C in
Bhopal division, settling 3.7°C to
4.7°C below seasonal norms.
Daytime temperatures also
dropped appreciably by 2.4°C to
3.9°C in Indore, Ujjain, and
Chambal divisions, hovering
2.1°C to 2.2°C below normal.
Seoni recorded the state’s lowest maximum temperature at
25.2°C, while Khajuraho registered the highest at 34.3°C. For
night temperatures, Khandwa
logged the state’s lowest minimum at 19.4°C, while Gwalior was
the highest at 26.7°C.
Accompanying the torrential
downpours were powerful winds
reaching 52 km/h in Raisen and
33 km/h in both Sehore and
Jabalpur. Thunderstorm and
lightning activity affected almost
every district across the state
except Morena. Meteorologists
trace this active spell to an upper
air cyclonic circulation over central parts of north MP extending
up to 7.6 km, the monsoon trough
running across Guna and
Damoh, an east-west shear
zone along 22°N latitude, and
a Western Disturbance interacting over northern latitudes.
Looking ahead, rain or drizzle with thunderstorms is forecast at most places in Bhopal,
Vidisha, Raisen, Sehore,
Rajgarh, Narmadapuram,
Betul, Harda, Ratlam, Ujjain,
Dewas, Shajapur, Agar,
Mandsaur, Neemuch, Guna,
Ashoknagar, Chhindwara,
Seoni, and Pandhurna. Heavy
rain alerts also cover isolated
parts of Raisen, Dhar, Ratlam,
Ujjain, Dewas, Shajapur,
Mandsaur, Guna, Ashoknagar,
Narsinghpur, Mandla, and
Balaghat. In Bhopal, the sky will
stay generally cloudy with
thunderstorms, lightning, and
rain expected throughout the
day. Maximum and minimum
temperatures are projected at
27°C and 24°C respectively,
with average wind speeds
between 12 and 14 km/h.

title: Gujarat Monsoon 2026: Red alert all over the state, why the monsoon is turning more intense? - Moneycontrol.com
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Gujarat Monsoon 2026: Red alert all over the state, why the monsoon is turning more intense?
The IMD has placed parts of Gujarat under a red alert as the southwest monsoon strengthens. Know what is driving the intense rainfall, the regions at risk and what the weather warning means.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red alert for parts of Gujarat as the southwest monsoon enters a more active phase. Extremely heavy rainfall has begun from July 23 in the state. Scientists warned of the possibility of flash floods, waterlogging and disruption to transport services.
Several parts of the state are expected to receive persistent rain as a strong weather system continues to influence western India.
What drives extreme rain in Gujarat?
Meteorologists say the current spell is being driven by a well-marked low-pressure system over western India. The system is pulling in large amounts of moisture from the Arabian Sea.
At the same time, the monsoon trough is favourably positioned over the region. This low-pressure belt helps warm, moist air rise into the atmosphere.As it gets cooler, rain-bearing clouds are formed causing rains for an extended period of time.
This all has resulted in strengthening of the monsoon in Gujarat.
Red Alert in the states, what is going on?
Red alert is the highest warning signal of the Indian Meteorological Department. It is declared whenever there is expected heavy rainfall and weather conditions affecting everyday life.
Valsad district in South Gujarat gets unprecedented rainfall with Umbergaon recording massive rainfall of 906 mm in 24 hours⚠️
Villages, roads, shops all inundated. Massive flood. Last 24 hours rainfall(in mm) Kaprada 731 Nanapondha 643 Dharampur 499 Pardi 448 Valsad City 403 pic.twitter.com/If5jd2vwyS — Viraj Kotian(MeteoClima🌏🌦🌩) (@kotian_viraj) July 23, 2026
The department has warned of possible urban flooding, overflowing rivers, poor visibility and disruption to road and rail transport. Residents in vulnerable areas have been advised to remain cautious.
Which areas are likely to be affected?
According to the IMD, the heaviest rainfall is expected over south Gujarat, central Gujarat and parts of Saurashtra and Kutch.
Neighbouring states like Maharashtra and Rajasthan may stay on alert due to heavy showers as the weather system moves northwards.
The IMD has urged people to avoid unnecessary travel during periods of intense rainfall. Those living in low-lying areas should monitor official weather updates and follow local advisories.
While the rain will help replenish reservoirs and support agriculture, it could also increase the risk of localised flooding over the coming days.

title: IMD issues yellow alert for heavy rain, thunderstorms in Odisha - orissapost.com
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Bhubaneswar: The regional centre of the India Meteorological Department (IMD) Wednesday issued a yellow warning for heavy rainfall, thunderstorms and gusty winds in several Odisha districts, including Puri, ahead of the Bahuda Yatra Friday.
According to the weather office, till 8.30 am of Friday, heavy rainfall is likely at one or two places in Baleshwar and Mayurbhanj. Thunderstorms accompanied by lightning and gusty surface winds reaching 30-40 kmph are also forecast in Baleshwar, Bhadrak, Jajpur, Kendrapada, Jagatsinghpur, Kataka, Sundargada, Jharsuguda, Baragada, Sambalpur, Debagada, Anugola, Dhenkanal, Kendujhar, Mayurbhanj, Puri and Khordha.
From Friday to 8.30 am of Saturday, the IMD has forecast heavy rainfall at isolated places in Baleshwar, Kendujhar and Mayurbhanj. Thunderstorms with lightning and wind speeds of up to 40 kmph are likely in Baleshwar, Bhadrak, Jajpur, Kendrapada, Jagatsinghpur, Kataka, Sundargada, Jharsuguda, Sambalpur, Kendujhar, Mayurbhanj, Gajapati, Ganjam, Puri, Khordha and Nayagada.

The weather office has advised people to closely monitor weather updates and avoid unnecessary travel during periods of intense rainfall or thunderstorm activity.
The IMD also urged residents, particularly those in rural areas, to stay away from open fields, water bodies, trees and other tall structures during lightning and strong winds to minimise the risk of accidents.
